1:
"And as they spake unto the people, the priests, and the captain of the temple, and the Sadducees, came upon them." The witnesses note the first organized opposition to the gospel. Chrysostom observes the swift succession of trials: "Ere yet they had time to take breath after their first trials, straightway they enter into others... the captain of the temple — the object again was to attach a public criminality to what was doing." He notes the rulers' increased boldness since the crucifixion: "sin, while it is yet struggling to the birth, is attended with some sense of shame; but when once fully born, it makes those more shameless."

On "the captain of the temple," the witnesses divide. Most (Lightfoot, Barnes, Robertson) identify him as the Levite commander of the temple guard. Robertson: "Twenty-four bands of Levites guarded the temple... The commander of each band was called captain. Josephus names this captain next to the high priest." But Calvin and Henry think him a Roman officer of the garrison in the tower Antonia. Calvin: "I do rather think that he was some chief captain of the Roman army... the Roman captain had the government of the temple, lest it should be a place of refuge for the Jews."

On the Sadducees, the witnesses note their special animus against the resurrection. Ellicott: "The higher members of the priesthood, Annas and Caiaphas, were themselves of this sect... Throughout the Acts the Sadducees are foremost as persecutors." Calvin: "because the question is about the resurrection, they set themselves against the apostles more than the rest."

The witnesses agree: as Peter and John taught, the priests, the captain of the temple, and the Sadducees came upon them — the first organized opposition to the gospel. The rulers, more shameless since the crucifixion (Chrysostom), sought to give a public, criminal character to the apostles' preaching. The "captain of the temple" was either the Levite commander of the temple guard (the majority view) or a Roman officer of the Antonia garrison (Calvin, Henry). The Sadducees, who denied the resurrection, were foremost in the opposition, galled that the apostles preached the resurrection through Jesus whom they had crucified. The verse opens the conflict between the infant church and the powers of darkness: the gospel, scarcely preached, at once provokes the spite of the religious authorities. Yet, as the following verses show, the persecution only spreads the word (v.4) — for the church's suffering days are often her growing days.
2:
"Being grieved that they taught the people, and preached through Jesus the resurrection from the dead." The witnesses note the cause of the rulers' grief. The grammatical voices stress the intensity of the word. Barnes: "It implies more than simple sorrow; it was a mingled emotion of indignation and anger... it was painful to them, or they could not bear it." Robertson: "Present passive participle of diaponeo... to be worked up, indignant." Clarke: "They were thoroughly fatigued with the continuance of this preaching."

The grief, the witnesses note, was twofold. First, at the resurrection doctrine: the Sadducees, denying the resurrection, "were troubled that the apostles adduced proof of it so strong as the resurrection of Jesus" (Barnes). Second, at the apostles' presumption in teaching: Barnes notes "the ground of their grief was as much the fact that they should presume to instruct the people... unlearned Galileans, unauthorized by them." Ellicott reads "preached in Jesus" as "in this as the crucial instance in which the resurrection of the dead had been made manifest."

The puritan Henry draws the lesson: "They were grieved at that which they should have rejoiced in, at that which angels rejoice in. Miserable is their case to whom the glory of Christ's kingdom is a grief; for, since the glory of that kingdom is everlasting, it follows that their grief will be everlasting too." The chief priests grieved that the resurrection was preached "through Jesus," giving him the honour.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the rulers were grieved — vexed with indignation and anger (Barnes, Robertson) — that the apostles taught the people and preached the resurrection through Jesus. The grief was twofold: the Sadducees galled at the resurrection doctrine they denied, and the chief priests galled both at the apostles' presumption in teaching without authority and at the honour given to Jesus whom they had crucified. The verse displays the misery of hearts to whom the glory of Christ's kingdom is a grief (Henry): they sorrowed at what angels rejoice in. Their grief at the gospel, far from a public calamity, was the envy of authority threatened and doctrine refuted. The resurrection of Jesus, proving him the Just One, brought his blood upon their heads (Wesley); and so they grieved where they should have repented. The verse marks the root of the persecution: not concern for truth, but indignation at the gospel's advance and the honour of the risen Christ.
3:
"And they laid hands on them, and put them in hold unto the next day: for it was now eventide." The brief verse records the arrest. The grammatical voices note the custody and the hour. Robertson: "In ward (eis teresin). Probably in one of the chambers of the temple. In safe keeping... Now eventide. Hence no trial could take place before the next day, a regulation violated in the case of Jesus." Ellicott: "the eventide began at 6 P.M.... in custody."

The puritan Henry draws the lesson of God's training: "See how God trains up his servants for sufferings by degrees, and by less trials prepares them for greater; now they resist unto bonds only, but afterwards to blood." The first imprisonment is the beginning of a discipline that would lead to martyrdom.

The detail that no trial could be held at evening the witnesses note as a contrast with the trial of Christ, which violated this regulation. The apostles are held in custody overnight, to be examined the next day — the lawful procedure, unlike the hasty night-trial of their Master. The night in prison the witnesses (with Calvin, under v.4) read as no hindrance to the gospel, which spread even while the preachers were bound.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter and John were arrested and held in custody overnight, since the evening hour forbade a trial till the next day. The grammatical voices note the lawful procedure (no night-trial), a pointed contrast with the unlawful haste shown in the trial of Christ (Robertson). Henry draws the lesson of God's gradual training of his servants for suffering: first bonds, afterward blood. The verse marks the first imprisonment of the apostles — the beginning of a long discipline of persecution by which God would prepare them for greater trials and at last for martyrdom. The night in prison, far from silencing the gospel, was the setting in which the word continued to prevail (v.4): the preachers were bound, but the word was not. The verse displays both the malice of the rulers, who could not bear the preaching, and the providence of God, who trains his servants for suffering by degrees and turns even their bonds to the furtherance of the gospel. The apostles, lately fled at the approach of danger, now stand firm in custody for the name of Christ.
4:
"Howbeit many of them which heard the word believed; and the number of the men was about five thousand." The witnesses note the gospel's triumph despite the arrest. Barnes draws the great lesson: "This was one of the instances which has since been so often repeated, in which persecution has only had a tendency to extend and establish the faith which it was designed to destroy. It finally came to be a proverb, that the blood of the martyrs is the seed of the church." The word prevailed even while the preachers were bound.

On the number, the witnesses divide. Calvin and Ellicott take "five thousand" as the aggregate of the whole church, not the new converts that day. Calvin: "I do not understand it of those which were newly added, but of the whole church." Ellicott: "became, or was made up to, about five thousand... the aggregate number of disciples." Clarke takes it cumulatively: "the one hundred and twenty... the three thousand... and one thousand eight hundred and eighty converted since." Wesley and Barnes note the men were counted apart from women and children (as at the feeding of the five thousand).

The Reformed Calvin marvels at the power: "the apostles are put in prison, but the force of their preaching is spread far and wide... a few men unarmed, furnished with no garrisons, do show forth more power in their voice alone, than all the world. Christ did more evidently declare by this efficacy and force of doctrine that he was alive, than if he should have offered his body to be handled."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: despite the arrest of the preachers, many who heard the word believed, and the number of the men reached about five thousand — a striking proof that persecution spreads rather than crushes the faith ("the blood of the martyrs is the seed of the church," Barnes). The witnesses divide on the number: the aggregate of the whole church (Calvin, Ellicott) or the cumulative total of converts (Clarke); the men counted apart from women and children. Calvin marvels at the power of the word: the preachers bound, yet the gospel spreading mightily — Christ proving himself alive more by this efficacy than by a visible appearance. The verse displays the unstoppable advance of the gospel against opposition: the church's suffering days are her growing days. The imprisonment of Peter and John could not silence the word; rather, the word prevailed, and the church grew to five thousand. The persecutors' attempt to crush the faith only extended it — a pattern repeated through all the church's history.
5:
"And it came to pass on the morrow, that their rulers, and elders, and scribes," The witnesses note the assembling of the Sanhedrin against the apostles. The grammatical and Reformation voices identify the three orders — rulers (chief priests and members of the council), elders (the heads of families and tribes), and scribes (the learned interpreters of the law) — the full Sanhedrin convened to try Peter and John.

The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the gravity of the assembly: the same great council, in the same place, and many of the same men who had condemned Christ, now convened against his apostles. The infant church faced the full weight of the nation's religious authority — the body that claimed to regulate the religious affairs of Israel.

The convening of the Sanhedrin the witnesses (with Wesley, under v.1) read as providentially ordered: Wesley noted "they should first bear a full testimony to the truth in the temple, and then in the great council; to which they could have had no access, had they not been brought before it as criminals." The arrest, far from silencing the apostles, gave them access to bear witness before the highest tribunal of the nation.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the trial scene. The witnesses agree: on the next day the rulers, elders, and scribes — the three orders of the Sanhedrin — assembled to try Peter and John. The full weight of the nation's religious authority, the same council and many of the same men who had condemned Christ, now convened against his apostles. Yet the assembly, far from silencing the gospel, providentially gave the apostles access to bear witness before the highest tribunal of the nation (Wesley) — a testimony they could not otherwise have made. The verse displays the gravity of the conflict: the infant church arraigned before the Sanhedrin. But what the rulers intended for the suppression of the gospel God overruled for its proclamation: Peter, filled with the Spirit (v.8), would preach Christ crucified and risen before the very council that crucified him. The trial scene opens with the full panoply of religious power arrayed against two unlearned Galileans — and the power of God in their testimony would confound it.
6:
"And Annas the high priest, and Caiaphas, and John, and Alexander, and as many as were of the kindred of the high priest, were gathered together at Jerusalem." The witnesses note the leading members of the council. On Annas and Caiaphas, Ellicott: "Annas, or Ananus, had been made high priest by Quirinus... and lived to see five of his sons occupy it after him. At this time, Joseph Caiaphas was the actual high priest... He had married the daughter of Annas; and the latter seems to have exercised a dominant influence." Robertson: "the title clung to Annas as here... Caiaphas so by Roman law, Annas so in the opinion of the Jews."

On "John," the witnesses offer a conjecture. Lightfoot, Clarke, and Ellicott suggest he may be Johanan ben Zaccai, "very famous at that time in the Jewish nation," reported to have been at the height of his fame forty years before the destruction of the Temple. Ellicott records the pathos of his deathbed words: "they are about to bring me before the King of kings... if He slay, it is eternal death; and Him I cannot soothe with words or appease with gifts." But the identification is "at best, uncertain."

On Alexander, the witnesses (Clarke, Ellicott) note a possible (but unsupported) identification with Alexander the brother of Philo, the Alabarch of Alexandria. On "the kindred of the high priest," Ellicott notes it may mean the personally related or "the heads of the four-and-twenty courses of priests" — all of whom "had probably taken part in our Lord's condemnation."

The thin comment matches the verse, a list of the council's leaders. The witnesses agree: the leading members of the Sanhedrin gathered — Annas (the ex-high priest still holding dominant influence and the title in Jewish eyes), Caiaphas (the actual high priest, his son-in-law), John (possibly the famous Johanan ben Zaccai, though uncertain), Alexander, and the high-priestly kindred. The same family that had condemned Christ now arraigned his apostles. The verse names the formidable array of religious power assembled against the infant church — the very men who had crucified the Lord. Luke "distinctly mentions all these, to show how formidable the enemies were against whom the infant Church of Christ had to contend" (Clarke). Yet before this assembly of the great and powerful, the unlearned Peter, filled with the Spirit, would boldly preach the risen Christ. The verse displays the magnitude of the opposition — the high-priestly dynasty, the council's leaders, the heads of the priestly courses — confronting two Galilean fishermen whose only power was the Spirit of God.
7:
"And when they had set them in the midst, they asked, By what power, or by what name, have ye done this?" The witnesses note the form of the trial and the question. Gill and Ellicott describe the setting: the Sanhedrin sat in a semicircle, the accused placed in the centre. Ellicott: "The president being in the middle of the arc, the accused standing in the centre."

On the question, the witnesses note its suspicious and contemptuous tone. Ellicott: "By what kind of power, or what kind of name? apparently in a tone of contempt... The question implied a suspicion that it was the effect of magic, or, as in the case of our Lord's casting out devils, by the power of Beelzebub. There is a touch of scorn in the way in which they speak of the thing itself. They will not as yet call it a sign or wonder, but have ye done this?" Clarke: "False prophets, reputed witches, wizards, etc., were to be brought before the sanhedrin... Hence they inquired by what power, by what supernatural energy; or in what name, by what mode of incantation."

The Reformed Calvin reads the question as feigned zeal: "they deal as if they were most earnest defenders and maintainers of God's glory... their importunateness is wonderful, in that they go about to drive the apostles to make denial, by asking many questions concerning a manifest matter, and to wring out by fear some other thing than they had confessed. But God doth bring their crafty wiliness to nought."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Sanhedrin, sitting in a semicircle with the accused in the centre, demanded "by what power or name" the apostles had done the miracle — a question in a tone of suspicion and contempt, implying the suspicion of magic or sorcery (Ellicott, Clarke), and refusing as yet to call the cure a "sign." The question, Calvin notes, was feigned zeal for God's glory, designed to intimidate the apostles into denial. The verse displays the malice of the council cloaked in the form of judicial inquiry: they would not investigate whether the miracle proved the apostles sent by God, but assumed imposture and sought to silence them by authority and fear. Yet the question, intended to entrap, gave Peter the opening to proclaim Christ. The contemptuous "have ye done this?" would be answered with the bold confession that it was done "by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom ye crucified" (v.10). The accusers' demand became the occasion of the gospel's proclamation before the highest court of the nation.
8:
"Then Peter, filled with the Holy Ghost, said unto them, Ye rulers of the people, and elders of Israel," The witnesses note Peter's Spirit-filled boldness, in striking contrast to his former cowardice. The grammatical voices stress the sudden inspiration. Ellicott: "The tense implies an immediate sudden inspiration, giving the wisdom and courage and words which were needed at the time. The promises of Matthew 10:19-20, Luke 21:14-15, were abundantly fulfilled." Clarke: now was fulfilled Christ's promise that "it is not ye that speak, but the Spirit of your Father."

The witnesses dwell on the transformation of Peter. Barnes: "That he was now able to stand boldly before this same tribunal evinced a remarkable change in his feelings... So deeply was the heart of Peter affected by his sin, and so genuine was his sorrow, that he doubtless remembered his crime on this occasion; and the memory of it inspired him with boldness." Ellicott: "A few weeks back Peter had quailed before the soldiers and servants in the high priest's palace. Now he stands before the Sanhedrin and speaks, in the language of respect, it is true, but also in that of unflinching boldness."

The Reformed Calvin attributes the boldness wholly to the Spirit: "seeing he had denied his Master, being afraid at the voice of a silly woman, he should have utterly fainted in such an assembly... unless he had been upholden by the power of the Spirit. He is another manner of man here than he was before." Barnes and Wesley note the respect Peter shows: "Ye rulers — he gives them the honour due to their office."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit (a sudden, fresh inspiration fulfilling Christ's promise, Matthew 10:19-20, Ellicott, Clarke), addressed the Sanhedrin with respect ("Ye rulers... and elders") yet with unflinching boldness. The transformation is striking: the man who had quailed before a servant girl and denied his Master now stands fearless before the great council that crucified him (Barnes, Ellicott). Calvin attributes the change wholly to the Spirit: he is "another manner of man here than he was before." The memory of his denial, Barnes suggests, inspired him to repair the evil by boldly confessing the Lord he had denied, before the same tribunal. The verse displays the power of the Spirit to transform a coward into a confessor: the trembling Peter is made the bold preacher, giving honour to the rulers yet fearlessly proclaiming Christ. His Spirit-filled boldness is both the fulfillment of Christ's promise and the proof of his genuine repentance — and the model of the confidence with which believers, filled with the Spirit, may witness before the powers of the world.
9:
"If we this day be examined of the good deed done to the impotent man, by what means he is made whole;" The witnesses note Peter's skillful turn of the charge. The grammatical voices observe the legal language. Ellicott: "The word is employed in its technical sense of a judicial interrogation... Of the good deed. Strictly, the act of beneficence. There is a manifest emphasis on the word as contrasted with the contemptuous this thing of the question." Robertson: "Note euergesia (benefactor, not a malefactor). Skilful turn made by Peter."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the irony: "Peter layeth tyranny to the charge of the priests and the scribes, because they examine them unjustly concerning a benefit which deserveth praise, as if he and his fellow had committed some heinous offense... Peter, being supported with this confidence, doth at the first gird them wittily with a taunting preface, because they sit as judges to condemn good deeds."

On "made whole," Ellicott notes the pregnant meaning: "The verb, as in our Lord's words, Thy faith hath made thee whole, has a pregnant, underlying meaning, suggesting the thought of a spiritual as well as bodily restoration." The word for healing (sozo) is also the word for salvation, preparing for the higher sense of v.12.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter skillfully turns the charge, noting that they are being examined for a "good deed" — an act of beneficence (euergesia), pointedly contrasted with the council's contemptuous "this thing" (Ellicott, Robertson). Calvin draws the irony: the apostles are arraigned as malefactors for a benefit that deserved praise, and Peter wittily girds the council for sitting as judges to condemn good deeds. The word "made whole" (sozo), the same as for salvation, carries a pregnant undertone of spiritual as well as bodily restoration (Ellicott), preparing for the proclamation of salvation in Christ alone (v.12). The verse displays Peter's masterful confession: he frames the charge so that the council is shown condemning a work of mercy, and turns the inquiry into an occasion to preach Christ. The "good deed" done to the lame man becomes the text for the gospel of the greater salvation. Peter, far from defending himself as a malefactor, presses the council with the absurdity of trying men for healing — and moves at once to the name by which the healing was wrought and salvation is found.
10:
"Be it known unto you all, and to all the people of Israel, that by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom ye crucified, whom God raised from the dead, even by him doth this man stand here before you whole." The witnesses note Peter's bold confession of Christ crucified and risen. Barnes marvels at the boldness: "Although this frank and open avowal was attended with danger, and although it was in the presence of the great and the mighty, yet he chose to state fully and clearly his conviction of the truth. Never was there an instance of greater boldness." He notes the transformation: "The timid, trembling, yielding Simon... is now transformed into the manly, decided, and firm Cephas."

The grammatical voices note the pointed charges. Robertson: "Whom ye crucified. Too good a chance to miss, and so Peter boldly charges the Sanhedrin with responsibility for the death of Jesus... Whom God raised from the dead. This is God's answer to their act of crucifixion." Ellicott: "He presses home the fact that, though Pilate had given the formal sentence, it was they who had crucified their King. He proclaims that He has been raised from the dead, and is still as a Power working to heal."

The Reformed Calvin notes the sequence: "he maketh Christ the author of the miracle... he testifieth that Christ is alive, because God hath raised him up from the dead, howsoever they had crucified him. So that the miracle giveth him occasion to preach the resurrection of Christ." He notes Peter charges them with the crucifixion "not only to upbraid this unto them, that they may acknowledge their fault; but also that they may understand that they have in vain striven against God."

The witnesses agree: Peter boldly confesses that the lame man stands whole "by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom ye crucified, whom God raised from the dead." The confession is doubly bold: it names the despised Nazarene as the Christ (which the council denied), and charges the Sanhedrin itself with his crucifixion, contrasting their act with God's answer in the resurrection (Robertson, Ellicott). The transformed Peter, lately a coward, now fearlessly presses the charge of the crucifixion home upon the very men who committed it. The miracle gives him occasion to preach the resurrection — the proof that Jesus is the Christ (Calvin). The verse displays the heart of the apostolic preaching: Christ crucified by men, raised by God, and now working salvation. Peter charges the rulers with their crime not merely to upbraid but to call them to repentance and to show that they have striven against God in vain. The healed man standing before them is the living proof of the power of the risen Christ.
11:
"This is the stone which was set at nought of you builders, which is become the head of the corner." The witnesses note Peter's citation of Psalm 118:22, applied to Christ. The grammatical voices trace the prophecy. Barnes: "The ancient Jews applied this to David... The New Testament writers apply it without any doubt to the Messiah... God will overrule the devices and plans of wicked men to accomplish his own purposes. What men despise and set at nought, he esteems of inestimable value." Robertson: "The experts, the architects, had rejected Jesus for their building as Jesus himself had pointed out (Matthew 21:42). This very Rejected Stone God had made the head of the corner."

Ellicott notes the pointed application to the council: "The members of the Council to whom Peter spoke had heard those words quoted and interpreted before. They, by their calling, the builders of the Church of Israel, did reject the stone which God had chosen to be the chief corner-stone." He notes the echo in Peter's epistle (1 Peter 2:6-8).

The Reformed Calvin draws the polemic against false church-authority: "it is no new thing that the ringleaders of the Church... have wickedly rejected Christ... Whereby the vain boasting of the Pope is plainly refuted, who maketh his boast of the bare title... they can go no farther at length than to be called chief builders with Annas and Caiaphas." The rejection of Christ by the official builders is no proof against him, but rather the fulfillment of prophecy; and his exaltation by God, despite their rejection, is the believer's comfort.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter cites Psalm 118:22 — "the stone the builders rejected is become the head of the corner" — applying it pointedly to the council. They, the official "builders" of Israel's church, had rejected the stone God chose to be the chief corner-stone (Christ), fulfilling the very prophecy they had heard and interpreted (Ellicott, Robertson). The verse displays God's overruling of the wicked: what the builders set at nought, God exalted to the head of the corner. Calvin draws the lesson against false church-authority: the rejection of Christ by the official rulers is no argument against him but the fulfillment of Scripture, and a warning that mere title (whether of Annas, Caiaphas, or the Pope) is no guarantee of faithfulness. The believer's comfort is that Christ, rejected by the builders, is exalted by God to the chief place, and his honour shall remain safe though the rulers rage. The Rejected Stone is the foundation of the church; and those who reject him build in vain.
12:
"Neither is there salvation in any other: for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved." The witnesses note this great declaration of the exclusivity of salvation in Christ. Gill: "Christ is the only Saviour and Redeemer... nor is salvation to be sought and hoped for from any other; not in a man's self, nor in any other creature... not by obedience to the law of Moses, moral or ceremonial." Robertson: "It is amazing to see Peter speaking thus to the Sanhedrin... There is no second name to go beside that of Jesus in India, China, Japan, or America."

Barnes expounds the doctrine and its scope, including a long excursus on the heathen and infants: "That there is no other Saviour, or Mediator between God and man, is abundantly taught in the New Testament; and it is indeed the main design of revelation to prove this." On infants and the heathen who are saved, he holds it is "in consequence of the intervention of Christ" — the Name working even where unknown. Ellicott concurs: "Where it is not so known, it rises to its higher significance as the symbol of a divine energy; and so we may rightly say that the heathen who obtain salvation are saved by the Name of the Lord of whom they have never heard."

The Reformed Calvin presses the exclusivity against all rival means: "The Papists confess with us that salvation is in God alone, but by and by they forge to themselves infinite ways to attain unto the same. But Peter calleth us back unto Christ alone... when Peter excludeth plainly all other means, he placeth perfect salvation in Christ alone, and not some part thereof only." Wesley notes the "beautiful gradation" from the cripple's bodily healing to the salvation of sinful souls.

The witnesses agree: Peter declares the great truth that salvation is found in no other than Christ — "there is none other name under heaven given among men whereby we must be saved." This is the exclusivity of salvation in Christ alone, against all rival means: not the law, not works, not any creature, but Christ only (Gill, Calvin). Calvin presses it against the Papists' multiplied ways of salvation; Peter places perfect and whole salvation in Christ alone. On the heathen and infants who are saved, the witnesses (Barnes, Ellicott) hold it is by the Name even where unknown — the Name rising to its higher significance as the symbol of divine saving energy. The verse displays the heart of the gospel and the apostolic claim: there is salvation in no other. The "beautiful gradation" (Wesley) rises from the lame man's bodily healing to the salvation of the soul, both wrought by the one Name. The verse remains the church's confession before all the world: no second name beside Jesus, in any land or age, by which men must be saved.
13:
"Now when they saw the boldness of Peter and John, and perceived that they were unlearned and ignorant men, they marvelled; and they took knowledge of them, that they had been with Jesus." The witnesses note the council's amazement at the boldness of unlearned men. The grammatical voices define the terms. Ellicott: "Unlearned... means literally, unlettered... not having been educated as a scribe, not having studied the Law... The second word means literally, a private person, one without special office or calling... It passed into modern languages with the idea of ignorance, and so acquired its later sense of idiot." Robertson clarifies: "It does not mean ignorant, but a layman, a man not in office... Peter and John were men of ability and of courage, but they did not belong to the set of the rabbis."

On "boldness," Robertson notes "Actually Peter had turned the table on the Sanhedrin and had arraigned them before the bar of God." On "took knowledge," Ellicott: "they began to recognise... one after another of the rulers began to remember the persons of the two Apostles as they had seen them with their Master in the Temple."

Barnes guards against misusing this as a charter for an unlearned ministry: "Christ himself expressed his opposition to an unlearned ministry, by teaching them himself... and then by bestowing on them miraculous endowments which no learning at present can furnish. In the single selection of an apostle after his ascension... he chose a learned man, the apostle Paul." Yet the central lesson is that boldness comes from conviction: "Boldness and success in the ministry will depend far more on honest, genuine, thorough conviction of the truth, than on all the endowments of talent and learning."

The witnesses agree: the council marvelled at the boldness of Peter and John, "unlearned and ignorant men" — that is, not scribes nor men of official rank (laymen without rabbinic training, Ellicott, Robertson) — and recognized them as having "been with Jesus." The boldness of these untrained men, who had turned the tables and arraigned the council before God (Robertson), astonished the rulers. Barnes guards against the misuse of this as a charter for an unlearned ministry: Christ himself taught the apostles, endowed them with the Spirit, and chose the learned Paul; their boldness sprang not from ignorance but from thorough conviction born of having been with Jesus. The verse displays the power of conviction over mere learning: men of no schools, but full of the Spirit and the certainty of what they had seen, confounded the great council. The mark "they had been with Jesus" is the secret of their boldness — and the abiding lesson that fellowship with Christ, more than human endowments, makes the bold and successful witness. Those who have been with Jesus cannot but speak with power.
14:
"And beholding the man which was healed standing with them, they could say nothing against it." The brief verse records the council's helplessness before the evidence of the miracle. Barnes: "The presence of the man that was healed was an unanswerable fact in proof of what the apostles alleged. The miracle was so public, clear, and decisive; the man that was healed was so well known, that there was no evasion or subterfuge by which they could escape the conclusion." He notes the man's gratitude in standing with his benefactors.

Clarke draws the logical force: "If the doctrine be false, the man cannot have been miraculously healed: if the man be miraculously healed, then the doctrine must be true that it is by the name of Jesus of Nazareth that he has been healed. But the man is incontestably healed; therefore the doctrine is true." The undeniable miracle silenced the council.

The grammatical voices note the council's enforced silence. Robertson: "Imperfect again, they kept on having nothing to say against it. The lame man was standing there before their eyes in proof of what Peter had said." The healed man, standing whole with the apostles, was living, irrefutable proof.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the council could say nothing against the apostles, for the healed man stood before them — living, irrefutable proof of the miracle (Barnes, Robertson). The miracle was too public and decisive to deny; and Clarke draws the logical force: the undeniable healing proved the doctrine that it was wrought by the name of Jesus. The man's standing with his benefactors showed his gratitude and added to the testimony. The verse displays the helplessness of unbelief before the evidence of God's work: the council, unable to deny the fact, is reduced to silence. The healed man, whole and present, confounded the rulers more than any argument could. The verse marks the triumph of the gospel's evidence over the malice of its enemies: the miracle stands, the doctrine is proved, and the council can only consult how to suppress what they cannot refute (v.16). The living trophy of Christ's power silences the highest court of the nation; and the truth, attested by an undeniable work, leaves the accusers without an answer.
15:
"But when they had commanded them to go aside out of the council, they conferred among themselves," The witnesses note the council's private consultation. The puritan Henry observes the dilemma: the rulers, confronted with an undeniable miracle and bold preaching they could not answer, withdrew to confer how to suppress what they could not refute. The verse marks their turning from judicial inquiry to political calculation.

The grammatical voices note that Luke records what passed in the closed session — perhaps known through a member like Nicodemus or Joseph of Arimathea, or Paul (then Saul). The council, having put the apostles out, debated among themselves, no longer weighing the evidence but seeking a way to stop the spread of the gospel.

The consultation the witnesses read as exposing the council's bad faith. Having no answer to the miracle or the doctrine, they sought not the truth but their own authority. The private conference, recorded by Luke, reveals the rulers' helplessness and malice: they could neither deny the fact nor refute the doctrine, yet were determined to suppress it.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the council's private deliberation. The witnesses agree: the rulers, unable to answer the miracle or the preaching, put the apostles out and conferred privately how to deal with them — turning from judicial inquiry to political calculation (Henry). Luke records what passed in the closed session, perhaps through a sympathetic member. The verse exposes the council's bad faith: having no answer to the evidence, they sought not the truth but the preservation of their own authority and the suppression of the gospel. The private conference reveals their helplessness and malice — they could neither deny the fact nor refute the doctrine, yet were resolved to silence it. The verse marks the transition from the apostles' bold testimony to the council's furtive scheming: the powers of the world, confounded by the truth, retreat to consult how they may oppose what they cannot overthrow. The closed-door deliberation of the Sanhedrin, recorded for the church, shows the impotence of opposition against the gospel: they can only ask "what shall we do to these men?" (v.16), having no power to refute or deny.
16:
"Saying, What shall we do to these men? for that indeed a notable miracle hath been done by them is manifest to all them that dwell in Jerusalem; and we cannot deny it." The witnesses note the council's admission of the miracle and their dilemma. Ellicott exposes the abandonment of justice: "The question now debated was clearly one that never ought to have been even asked. They were sitting as a Court of Justice, and should have given their verdict according to the evidence. They abandon that office, and begin discussing what policy was most expedient."

The grammatical voices note the forced confession. Robertson: "Notable miracle (or sign). It was useless to deny it with the man there... We cannot deny it. That is, it will do no good." Ellicott: "The very form of the sentence betrays the will, though there is not the power." Clarke: "all Jerusalem knew that he was lame... and now all Jerusalem knew that he was healed; and there was no means by which such a self-evident fact could be disproved."

The admission the witnesses read as the involuntary testimony of the enemies to the truth. The council, unable to deny a miracle known to all Jerusalem, confesses it — yet, instead of submitting to the God who wrought it, debates how to suppress its effect. Their own words convict them: they acknowledge the manifest work of God and oppose it still.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the council confesses the miracle — "a notable miracle hath been done... manifest to all, and we cannot deny it" — yet debates not justice but expediency (Ellicott). They abandon their office as a court, no longer weighing evidence but calculating policy. The forced admission is the involuntary testimony of the enemies to the truth (Clarke, Robertson): unable to deny a work known to all Jerusalem, they confess it, yet seek to suppress its effect rather than submit to the God who wrought it. The verse displays the self-condemnation of unbelief: the council acknowledges the manifest work of God and opposes it still. Their own words ("we cannot deny it") convict them of warring against God with open eyes. The miracle is confessed by its enemies; the doctrine is unrefuted; and the rulers, having no ground for justice, descend to threats (v.17). The verse marks the impotence and bad faith of opposition: the truth is admitted, yet the heart, hardened, will not yield. They confess the sign and resolve to silence the witnesses.
17:
"But that it spread no further among the people, let us straitly threaten them, that they speak henceforth to no man in this name." The witnesses note the council's resort to threats. The grammatical voices explain the Hebraism. Ellicott: "The Greek gives literally, let us threaten them with threats. The phrase gives the Hebrew idiom for expressing intensity by reduplication... indicates that St. Luke translated from a report of the speech which Caiaphas had delivered in Aramaic." Robertson notes the contempt: "They will not say Jesus, but make a slur at this name, contemptuous use of houtos."

Clarke explains the motive: "if this teaching should be permitted to go on, probably accompanied with similar miracles, they had reason to believe that all Jerusalem... should be converted to the religion of him whom they had lately crucified." And the special offense of the name: "Nothing so ominous to them as the name of Christ crucified, because they themselves had been his crucifiers."

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on the limits of authority abused: "when that religion and reverence which ought doth not reign, the more holy the place is which a man doth possess, the more boldly doth he rage... if they abuse their honor, the Spirit declareth there, as in a glass, what small account we ought to make of their decrees and commandments... if they dare be so bold, we may lawfully refuse to obey them."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the council, unable to deny the miracle, resorts to threats — "let us threaten them with threats" (a Hebraism of intensity, indicating Luke translated from an Aramaic report, Ellicott) — commanding the apostles to speak no more "in this name" (a contemptuous slur at the name of Jesus, Robertson). The motive was to stop the spread of the gospel, lest all Jerusalem be converted to him whom they had crucified (Clarke); and the special offense was the name of Christ crucified, ominous to his crucifiers. Calvin draws the lesson on authority abused: when reverence for God fails, the holiest office rages most boldly; and the believer may lawfully refuse to obey commands that oppose God. The verse displays the impotence of mere power against the truth: having no answer, the council can only threaten. The command to silence, which the apostles would defy (vv.19-20), marks the first attempt to suppress the gospel by force — the resort of an authority that has lost the argument and abandoned justice.
18:
"And they called them, and commanded them not to speak at all nor teach in the name of Jesus." The brief verse records the formal prohibition. The grammatical voices note its sweeping force. Ellicott: "The Greek is even more forcible: absolutely not to utter... The very name of Jesus was not to pass their lips." Wesley: "They charged them not to speak privately; nor teach publicly." The prohibition was total — neither private speech nor public teaching in the name.

Clarke draws out the special hatred of the name: "Any other doctrine, and any other name, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites and infidels will bear, but the doctrine which is according to godliness, proclaiming salvation through the blood of Christ crucified, they will not bear. If their doctrine were not the truth of God it could not be so unpopular; there is such an enmity in human nature against all that is good and true, that whatever comes from God is generally rejected by wicked men."

The prohibition the witnesses read as the council's desperate attempt to silence the gospel by command. Having no answer to the miracle or the doctrine, they forbid the very name of Jesus — an order that the apostles would at once refuse (vv.19-20). The total ban on the name marks the council's recognition that the power lay in the name they could not bear.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the council formally commanded the apostles not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus — a total prohibition, the very name not to pass their lips (Ellicott, Wesley). Clarke draws out the special enmity: men will bear any doctrine but the gospel of salvation through Christ crucified, for human nature is at enmity with what comes from God. The verse displays the council's desperate resort to command: having no answer to the truth, they forbid the name itself. The total ban marks their recognition that the power lay in the name of Jesus, which they could not bear and could not refute. Yet the prohibition would be at once defied (vv.19-20): the apostles, bound to obey God rather than men, could not but speak the things they had seen and heard. The verse sets the stage for the great confession of the liberty of conscience: the powers of the world forbid the name of Christ, and the witnesses of Christ refuse to be silenced. The name the council banned is the name by which alone men must be saved (v.12).
19:
"But Peter and John answered and said unto them, Whether it be right in the sight of God to hearken unto you more than unto God, judge ye." The witnesses note the great principle of liberty of conscience. Barnes draws out its historic import: "this is one of the first and most bold appeals on record in favour of the right of private judgment and the liberty of conscience... the same spirit manifested itself thus early in the Christian church against all dominion over the conscience, and in favour of the right to follow the dictates of the conscience and the will of God." The apostles appeal directly to the council as judges: would God approve obeying men rather than him?

Clarke paraphrases the apostles' resolve: "Worldly prudence would induce us to obey you; but acting as before God, and following the dictates of eternal truth, we dare not be silent. Can it be right to obey men contrary to the command and will of God?" Ellicott carefully qualifies the principle: it asserts "the right of conscience, recognising a divine authority, to resist a human authority which opposes it," but notes that "the onus probandi rests on the man who asserts the higher authority," especially where the question is one of witness to facts attested by signs.

The Reformed Calvin draws the limit of church authority: "the obedience which men use toward evil and unfaithful pastors, howsoever they hold the lawful government of the Church, is contrary to God... we must examine all their traditions by the rule of the Word of God. We must obey princes and others which are in authority, yet so that they rob not God of his right." Wesley compares Socrates: "I will obey God rather than you."

The witnesses agree: Peter and John assert the great principle of liberty of conscience — appealing to the council itself to judge whether it is right to obey men rather than God. This is one of the earliest and boldest appeals for the right of private judgment and the liberty of conscience against all dominion over the soul (Barnes). The apostles, acting before God, dare not be silent at man's command (Clarke). The witnesses carefully bound the principle: it is the right of a conscience recognizing divine authority to resist a human authority that opposes it (Ellicott); the limit of church and civil authority is that they must not rob God of his right (Calvin). The verse displays the foundation of Christian liberty: God is to be obeyed above all human powers, and no magistrate, council, or church may bind the conscience against the will of God. The apostles' bold appeal — "judge ye" — leaves the council convicted by its own conscience, and stands as the charter of the believer's freedom to obey God before men.
20:
"For we cannot but speak the things which we have seen and heard." The witnesses note the apostles' necessity to bear witness. The grammatical voices note the double negative. Robertson: "Both negatives hold here, For we are not able not to speak (what we saw and heard). This is defiance of the civil and ecclesiastical authorities that was justified, for the temple authorities stepped in between the conscience and God... This is the courage of martyrs through all the ages." Ellicott: "The question at issue was one of bearing witness, and that witness they had received a special command to bear (Acts 1:8)."

Barnes uses this verse for a long excursus on religious liberty, tracing its history from the Jews' love of freedom, through the early martyrs, to the Reformation and the spread of the Bible: "If we love liberty; if we hate tyranny and superstition; if we wish to extend the knowledge of the rights of man... let us spread far and wide the Book of God." He states the principle: "Every man is responsible to God for his opinions and his conduct... The great question before every man is, What is right in the sight of God?"

The Reformed Calvin notes the apostles speak not generally but of the gospel: "the apostles do not speak generally, when as they say they cannot but speak. For the gospel of Christ is now in hand, wherein consisteth both the glory of God and the salvation of men... whosoever putteth them to silence, he endeavoreth to abolish the grace of God, and fordo the salvation of men. And if so be that a prohibition so wicked do stop our mouths, woe be to our sluggishness."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles declare they cannot but speak the things they have seen and heard — "we are not able not to speak" (the double negative, Robertson), the necessity of witnesses commanded to testify (Acts 1:8, Ellicott). This is the courage of martyrs and the foundation of religious liberty: the temple authorities stepped between the conscience and God, and the apostles justly defied them (Robertson). Barnes draws the long lesson of liberty — that every man is responsible to God for his opinions, and that the spread of the Bible spreads freedom. Calvin notes the necessity is specifically the gospel: to silence the witnesses of Christ is to abolish the grace of God and the salvation of men; and woe to the sluggishness that yields to so wicked a prohibition. The verse displays the holy necessity of witness: the apostles, having seen the risen Christ, cannot be silent at any human command. Loyalty to Christ counts more than safety; and the believer commissioned by God must speak, whatever the cost. The "we cannot but speak" of Peter and John is the watchword of every faithful witness.
21:
"So when they had further threatened them, they let them go, finding nothing how they might punish them, because of the people: for all men glorified God for that which was done." The witnesses note the council's helplessness and the people's praise. Barnes: "not being able to devise any way of punishing them, without exciting a tumult among the people, and endangering their own authority. The sanhedrim was frequently influenced by this fear; and it shows that their own authority was much dependent on the caprice of the multitude."

The grammatical voices note the people's continued praise. Robertson: "Imperfect active, kept on glorifying God while the Sanhedrin were threatening Peter and John. It was to laugh at the helplessness of the Sanhedrin." Ellicott notes this is "specially characteristic of St. Luke thus to note the impression made upon the people by signs and wonders." Clarke: the converts were now so numerous "that the sanhedrin was afraid to proceed to any extremities, lest an insurrection should be the consequence."

The Reformed Calvin reads the council's restraint as God's secret providence: "they are bridled by the secret power of God, so that they cannot tell how to do any hurt... the Lord doth bind them with his bonds, though they be ignorant thereof. Luke commendeth unto us the providence of God in preserving his children." Yet he notes the apostles get the victory "under the reproach of the cross," being threatened and charged again.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the council, after further threats, let the apostles go, unable to punish them for fear of the people, who glorified God for the miracle. The Sanhedrin's authority was dependent on the caprice of the multitude (Barnes); and the people's continued praise even amid the threats "was to laugh at the helplessness of the Sanhedrin" (Robertson). Calvin reads the council's restraint as God's secret providence binding the wicked with invisible bonds, so that they could do no harm — the preservation of God's children seen by faith. The verse displays the impotence of opposition and the protecting hand of God: the rulers, unable to deny the miracle or punish the apostles, are forced to release them, while the people glorify God. The gospel triumphs not by the apostles' power but by God's restraint of their enemies. Yet the victory is "under the cross" (Calvin): the apostles are threatened and charged, released but not vindicated by the world. The people's praise and the council's helplessness together proclaim that the work was of God, against which the rulers strove in vain.
22:
"For the man was above forty years old, on whom this miracle of healing was shewed." The brief verse notes the man's age, magnifying the miracle. Gill: "the miracle was the greater, that a man born lame, and who had been so above forty years, should have a cure; and he was the more known to the people, and his testimony met with more credit." Barnes: "after a continuance of forty years, all hope of healing him by ordinary means must have been abandoned; and all pretence that this was jugglery or deception must have been absurd."

The grammatical voices note Luke's characteristic precision. Ellicott: "This precision in noting the duration of disease or infirmity is again characteristic of the writer," comparing the woman with the issue of blood, Aeneas, and the cripple at Lystra. The exact noting of the man's age (over forty, lame from birth) is the physician-evangelist's attention to detail.

The age the witnesses read as confirming the greatness and genuineness of the miracle. A lifelong infirmity of over forty years, beyond all natural cure, made the healing unmistakably divine; and the man's long-known condition made his cure the more credible to the people. Clarke draws the application: "It is as easy with God to convert a sinner of forty or four-score, as one of ten years old."

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the man's age — over forty, lame from birth — magnifies the miracle. A lifelong infirmity beyond all natural cure made the healing unmistakably divine, and his long-known condition made the cure the more credible and the testimony the more weighty (Gill, Barnes). Luke's characteristic precision in noting the duration (the physician-evangelist's detail, Ellicott) underscores the genuineness of the miracle: no jugglery or deception could be alleged. The verse confirms the greatness of the work that silenced the council: a man lame for over forty years, healed in an instant by the name of Jesus. Clarke draws the spiritual application: as God easily healed the long-lame body, so he can convert the long-hardened soul, whatever its age. The verse closes the trial scene by underscoring the undeniable, divine character of the miracle — the living proof that confounded the rulers and moved the people to glorify God. The greatness of the cure matches the greatness of the name by which it was wrought.
23:
"And being let go, they went to their own company, and reported all that the chief priests and elders had said unto them." The witnesses note the apostles' return to the church and the report that prompts the great prayer. Chrysostom: "Not for their own glory did they tell the tale... but what they displayed was the proofs therein exhibited of the grace of Christ. All that their adversaries had said, this they told; their own part, it is likely, they omitted: this made the hearers all the more courageous."

The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the instinctive resort to the company of believers. Released, the apostles do not flee or scatter but go straight to "their own company" — the fellowship of the church — to report and to pray. The shared report and shared prayer mark the unity and mutual encouragement of the early church under persecution.

Chrysostom draws out the church's response: "These again flee to the true Succor, to the Alliance invincible, and again, with one accord... they lifted up their voice to God... with great earnestness, for it is no prayer made at random." The apostles' report becomes the occasion of the church's united prayer (vv.24-30) — not for vengeance or safety, but for boldness.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the section on the believers' prayer. The witnesses agree: the released apostles went straight to "their own company" — the fellowship of the church — and reported all the chief priests had said, not for their own glory but to display the grace of Christ and to encourage the hearers (Chrysostom). The instinctive resort to the company of believers, and the shared report, mark the unity and mutual encouragement of the persecuted church. The report becomes the occasion of the church's united prayer (vv.24-30), in which they flee to God, the invincible Ally, with earnest, ordered supplication (Chrysostom). The verse displays the response of faith to persecution: not flight or despair, but fellowship and prayer. The apostles, threatened and charged, do not retreat into silence but gather the church to seek God — and the prayer that follows asks not for vengeance or even safety, but for boldness to speak the word. The verse sets the pattern of the church under trial: to report to one another, to pray with one accord, and to seek not deliverance but courage in the cause of Christ.
24:
"And when they heard that, they lifted up their voice to God with one accord, and said, Lord, thou art God, which hast made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and all that in them is:" The witnesses note the church's united prayer and its opening address. The grammatical voices note the word for "Lord." Robertson: "O Lord (Despota). Our word despot. Old word for relation of master to slaves... Simeon thus addressed God (Luke 2:29). So in 2 Peter 2:1; Jude 1:4; Revelation 6:10." Ellicott: "Despotes, the absolute Master of the Universe... The hymn has the special interest of being the earliest recorded utterance of the praises of the Christian Church."

On the manner of the united prayer, Ellicott offers options: a direct inspiration suggesting the same words, the people following Peter clause by clause, or a familiar hymn — judging the second most probable. The "with one accord" (Luke's favourite phrase) marks the church's perfect unity in prayer.

The Reformed Calvin notes the foundation of the prayer in God's power as Creator: "they do in such sort acknowledge the power of God in the creation of the whole world, that they apply the same therewithal unto the present use... whatsoever dangers hang over their heads, yet can God prevent the same infinite ways, forasmuch as all things are in his hand." The appeal to the Creator's power is the ground of confidence against the threats of men.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the prayer. The witnesses agree: the church lifted up its voice to God with one accord, addressing him as "Despotes" — the absolute Sovereign Master of the universe (Robertson, Ellicott) — and the Creator of heaven, earth, and sea. This is the earliest recorded prayer of the Christian church (Ellicott), and it begins, like many Psalms, with the glory of God as Creator. Calvin notes the foundation: they appeal to the Creator's almighty power as the ground of confidence against the threats of men — for all things are in his hand, and he can prevent every danger. The "with one accord" (Luke's favourite phrase) marks the church's perfect unity in prayer. The verse displays the church's response to persecution: united, earnest prayer that begins not with their danger but with God's sovereignty. By addressing the Maker of all as absolute Master, they confess that the rulers who threaten them are themselves under his hand. The prayer's opening sets the tone: the Creator who made and governs all things is able to defend his servants and advance his kingdom against every opposition.
25:
"Who by the mouth of thy servant David hast said, Why did the heathen rage, and the people imagine vain things?" The witnesses note the church's citation of Psalm 2 and its testimony to inspiration. Barnes: "This is a strong, solemn testimony to the inspiration of David. It is a declaration of the apostles made in solemn prayer, that God spake himself by the mouth of David." The Psalm, applied to the Messiah, is invoked as prophecy now fulfilled.

The grammatical voices note the text and its meaning. Robertson: "By the mouth of our father David... From Psalm 2:1f here ascribed to David. Baumgarten suggests that the whole company sang the second Psalm and then Peter applied it to this emergency." Ellicott notes the variant reading "Who through the Holy Ghost, by the mouth of David our father, thy servant." Barnes explains "rage" (the tumultuous opposition of a mob) and "imagine vain things" (devising a plan that turns out vain, for they cannot oppose God's purpose).

The Reformed Calvin reads the Psalm's double reference: "We must need confess that David speaketh of himself, who after he was chosen king did enjoy the kingdom very hardly, because his enemies withstood him... but because his kingdom was established that it might be a figure of the kingdom of Christ, David doth not stay still in the shadow itself; but he apprehendeth the body." The opposition to David's kingdom prefigured the opposition to Christ's.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the church cites Psalm 2:1, ascribed to David by the inspiration of the Spirit (a solemn testimony to inspiration, Barnes) — "Why did the heathen rage?" — applying the prophecy to their present situation. Calvin reads the Psalm's double reference: David's hard-won kingdom, beset by enemies, prefigured the kingdom of Christ, against which the nations rage in vain. The "raging" is the tumultuous opposition of the wicked, the "vain things" their futile plans against God's purpose (Barnes). The verse displays the church's confidence grounded in prophecy: the very opposition they faced was foretold, and foretold to be vain. By invoking Psalm 2, the believers comfort themselves that the rage of the rulers against Christ and his church is no surprise to God but the fulfillment of his word — and, being foretold as vain, is doomed to fail. The Spirit who spoke through David spoke of this very hour; and the God who foresaw the opposition will surely overrule it for the establishing of his Son's kingdom.
26:
"The kings of the earth stood up, and the rulers were gathered together against the Lord, and against his Christ." The witnesses note the continuation of Psalm 2, applied to the opposition against Christ. Gill identifies the figures: "Herod Antipas... and Pilate the Roman governor... these stood, or rose up in an hostile manner, and set themselves against the Messiah." Robertson: "Against his Anointed (kata tou Christou autou). Against his Messiah, his Christ."

Barnes draws the great principle: "This passage implies that opposition to the Messiah is opposition to Jehovah. And this is uniformly supposed in the sacred Scriptures. He that is opposed to Christ is opposed to God. He that neglects him neglects God." He gives the reasons: Christ is the brightness of the Father's glory, equal with the Father, appointed by God, and dear to his heart; to oppose Christ is therefore to oppose God.

The Reformed Calvin presses the lesson: "all those do make war against God which refuse to submit themselves to Christ... because God will reign in the person of his Son alone, we refuse to obey him so often as we rebel against Christ... let the hypocrites profess a thousand times that they mean nothing less than to make war against God, yet shall they find this true, that God is their open enemy, unless they embrace Christ with his gospel."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the church continues Psalm 2 — "the kings of the earth stood up... against the Lord and against his Christ" — applying it to the opposition against the Messiah (Herod, Pilate, the rulers, Gill). The great principle, drawn by Barnes and Calvin, is that opposition to Christ is opposition to God: for Christ is equal with the Father, appointed by him, and dear to his heart; and God reigns in the person of his Son alone. To rebel against Christ is to make war against God, however the rebels protest otherwise (Calvin). The verse displays the church's confidence and the warning of the Psalm: the rulers who gathered against Christ gathered against God, and their war is therefore doomed. The believers comfort themselves that those who oppose them oppose not merely men but the Lord and his Anointed — and so cannot prevail. The verse both grounds the church's prayer in prophecy and proclaims the solemn truth that all who reject Christ set themselves against God, who will establish his Son's kingdom against every foe.
27:
"For of a truth against thy holy child Jesus, whom thou hast anointed, both Herod, and Pontius Pilate, with the Gentiles, and the people of Israel, were gathered together," The witnesses note the church's application of Psalm 2 to the actual conspirators against Christ. On "child" (better, "servant"), Barnes: "The word which is used here pais is different from that which is commonly applied... and the word son would have been a more happy translation." Ellicott and Clarke prefer "Servant," matching the use of David in v.25.

The grammatical voices note the four parties matching the Psalm. Robertson: "Luke alone (Luke 23:12) tells of the reconciliation between Herod and Pilate at the trial of Jesus. So Peter and the rest interpret this prophecy as directly fulfilled in their conduct towards Jesus Christ." Ellicott notes the "undesigned coincidence" with Luke's account of Herod's part in the Passion. The "kings" of the Psalm are Herod and Pilate; the "Gentiles" the Romans; the "peoples" Israel.

The Reformed Calvin draws the comfort: "they declare that this prophecy was proved to be true by the event, to the end they may believe the same more assuredly... it is expedient for us to apply the events of things which are foretold to the confirmation of our faith." Though the enemies seemed to triumph in Christ's death, the faithful confess their rage was vain, for they could do no more than God appointed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the church applies Psalm 2 to the actual conspirators against "thy holy servant Jesus, whom thou hast anointed" — Herod and Pilate (the kings), the Gentiles (Rome), and the people of Israel, gathered together against Christ. The four parties exactly match the Psalm (Robertson), with an undesigned coincidence in Herod's part recorded only by Luke (Ellicott). The word "child" is better "Servant" (Barnes, Ellicott), matching David in v.25. Calvin draws the comfort: the prophecy was proved true by the event, confirming faith; and though the enemies seemed to triumph in the crucifixion, their rage was vain, for they could do no more than God appointed (v.28). The verse displays the church's interpretation of Christ's passion as the fulfillment of prophecy: the very enemies who conspired against the Anointed fulfilled, unwittingly, the counsel of God. The believers find confidence in this — that the opposition they now face, like the opposition that crucified Christ, is foretold, governed, and overruled by God for his own purpose.
28:
"For to do whatsoever thy hand and thy counsel determined before to be done." The witnesses note the profound statement of divine sovereignty over the crucifixion. Gill: "it was not the end of their gathering together against Christ... to fulfil the purposes and decrees of God, but to fulfil their own lusts... but it was so in the event, according to the wise disposal of providence, that by their gathering together... they brought about what God in his everlasting council had decreed." The wicked acted freely from their own malice, yet fulfilled God's predetermined counsel.

The grammatical voices note the providence-and-freedom problem. Ellicott: "The great problem of the relation of the divine purpose to man's free agency is stated, without any attempt at a philosophical solution. No such solution is indeed possible... we must follow the example of the Apostle, and hold both the facts... 'Man proposes, God disposes.' 'God works in us, therefore we must work.'" Robertson: "They rise above sight and seem to see the Hand which shapes men's ends, rough hew them how they will" (Furneaux).

The Reformed Calvin presses the strong sovereignty: "God doth so govern and guide all things by his secret counsel, that he doth bring to pass those things which he hath determined, even by the wicked. Not that they are ready willingly to do him such service, but because he turneth their counsels and attempts backward... we must so consider the providence of God, that we know that it is the chief and only guider of all things which are done in the world." He notes the verse refutes those who admit only foreknowledge: God "hath appointed before that thing to be done which was done."

The witnesses agree: the church confesses that the conspirators against Christ did "whatsoever thy hand and counsel determined before to be done" — the profound statement of divine sovereignty over the crucifixion. The wicked acted freely from their own malice, yet fulfilled God's predetermined counsel (Gill). The witnesses divide on how to hold this: Ellicott states the problem of providence and free agency without solution, holding both facts ("Man proposes, God disposes"); Calvin presses the strong sovereignty, that God governs all things by his secret counsel, bringing to pass what he determined even through the wicked, refuting those who admit only foreknowledge. The verse displays the deepest mystery of the gospel: the greatest crime in history, the crucifixion of the Son, was at once the free act of wicked men and the determined counsel of God for the salvation of the world. The church finds comfort in this — that even the rage of Christ's enemies served God's purpose, and so the opposition they themselves faced was under God's sovereign hand. What men meant for evil, God meant for good; and the Hand that shaped the crucifixion shapes all things.
29:
"And now, Lord, behold their threatenings: and grant unto thy servants, that with all boldness they may speak thy word," The witnesses note the church's remarkable petition — not for safety, but for boldness. Barnes: "They did not purpose to abandon their undertaking; they resolved to persevere... With this purpose they implored the protection of God; they asked that he would not suffer them to be deterred from speaking boldly." Clarke: "It is not against us, but against thee, that they conspire... Now, Lord, look upon it; consider this."

Chrysostom (under v.23) had marvelled at the largeness of mind: "These are not words of imprecation... they do not say, Crush them, cast them down; but what? Grant unto Thy servants, that with all boldness they may speak Thy word. Let us also learn thus to pray." The church, threatened with death, prays not for vengeance or even deliverance, but for courage to speak.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson: "they crave at God's hands that he will beat down the cruelty of the adversaries; yet not so much for their own sake that they may live quietly... as that they may have liberty to preach the gospel in all places... Grant unto thy servants, O Lord, that they may speak boldly." The petition is for the furtherance of the gospel, not for personal ease.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the church, having grounded its prayer in God's sovereignty (vv.24-28), now asks God to behold the threatenings and to grant — not safety or vengeance, but boldness to speak his word. This is the remarkable largeness of mind that Chrysostom marvels at: threatened with death, they pray not "Crush them" but "Grant us boldness." Barnes notes they resolved to persevere, and asked only that they not be deterred. Calvin draws the lesson: they seek not their own quiet but the liberty to preach the gospel everywhere. The verse displays the heroic spirit of the persecuted church: their concern is not for their own safety but for the furtherance of the word. They commit their danger to God ("behold their threatenings") and ask for the courage to keep speaking. The petition rebukes the timid prayer of self-preservation and models the prayer of those wholly devoted to Christ's cause: not "deliver us," but "embolden us." The church under threat seeks not escape but faithfulness — and asks God for the boldness to speak his word whatever the cost.
30:
"By stretching forth thine hand to heal; and that signs and wonders may be done by the name of thy holy child Jesus." The witnesses note the church's prayer for continued miracles to confirm the word. Barnes: "The apostles not only desired boldness to speak, but they asked that God would continue to work miracles, and thus furnish to them, and to the people, evidence of the truth of what they delivered. They did not even ask that he would preserve their lives... They were intent on their work."

The grammatical voices note the wordplay. Ellicott: "There seems something like an intentional assonance in the Greek words which St. Luke uses — iâsis (healing) and Jesus — as though he would indicate that the very name of Jesus witnessed to His being the great Healer." On "child," again better "Servant" (Clarke, Ellicott).

The Reformed Calvin draws out the church's zeal for God's glory: "they make so great account of the glory of God, that in comparison of this, they set light by all other things. They have respect unto this one thing only, that the power of God may be declared by miracles, which the godly ought always to desire, although the adversaries burst." The prayer for signs is a prayer for the confirmation and spread of the gospel, not for self-vindication.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which completes the prayer. The witnesses agree: the church asks God to stretch forth his hand to heal and to do signs and wonders by the name of his holy Servant Jesus — not for their own safety, but for the confirmation and spread of the gospel (Barnes). They are intent on their work, not their lives. Ellicott notes the assonance of "healing" (iasis) and "Jesus," as if the very name witnessed to the great Healer. Calvin draws out their zeal for God's glory: they desire above all that God's power be declared by miracles, confirming the word. The verse displays the singular devotion of the praying church: their petition is for boldness to speak (v.29) and for power to confirm the word — both for the glory of God and the advance of the gospel. They ask not to be spared but to be made effective; not for deliverance but for the demonstration of God's power in the name of Jesus. The prayer, asking signs to attest the word, would be answered at once (v.31) by the shaking of the place and a fresh filling with the Spirit. The church's whole concern is the furtherance of Christ's name and kingdom.
31:
"And when they had prayed, the place was shaken where they were assembled together; and they were all filled with the Holy Ghost, and they spake the word of God with boldness." The witnesses note the immediate answer to the prayer. Barnes: "The event which followed was regarded by them as an evidence that God heard their prayer. The place was shaken... a convulsion so great, sudden, and mighty, was fitted at that time to awe them with a proof of the presence and power of God." Robertson: "By an earthquake most likely, but none the less a token of God's presence and power."

On the filling with the Spirit, the grammatical voices note it as a fresh endowment for boldness. Barnes: "Their being filled with the Holy Ghost, here, rather denotes their being inspired with confidence or boldness." Clarke: "Though these disciples had received the Holy Spirit on the day of pentecost, yet they were capable of larger communications... one communication of this Spirit always makes way for another. Neither apostle nor private Christian can subsist in the Divine life without frequent influences from on high."

The Reformed Calvin marks the twofold answer: "God did not only hear this prayer, but did also testify the same by a visible sign... the shaking of the place... is nothing else but a token of the presence of God. But the fruit followeth, for they are all filled with the Holy Ghost, and endowed with greater boldness. We ought rather to stand upon this second member." The shaking attested God's presence; the fresh boldness was the substance of the answer.

The witnesses agree: the prayer was answered at once — the place was shaken (an earthquake, a token of God's presence and power, Barnes, Robertson), and the believers were all filled afresh with the Holy Spirit and spoke the word with boldness. The shaking attested God's presence; the fresh filling and boldness were the substance of the answer to their petition (v.29). Clarke draws the lesson: though they had received the Spirit at Pentecost, they were capable of larger communications, for the Christian needs frequent fresh influences from on high. Calvin marks the twofold answer: the visible sign of God's presence, and the fruit — greater boldness. The verse displays the immediate, gracious answer to the united prayer of the church: they asked for boldness, and received it, with a visible token of God's presence. The prayer for courage is answered by the very gift sought; and the church, freshly filled with the Spirit, goes forth to speak the word with the boldness it had asked. The shaken place and the fresh filling are God's seal upon the prayer of his persecuted people — and the pattern of how the church, seeking not safety but faithfulness, is empowered to speak.
32:
"And the multitude of them that believed were of one heart and of one soul: neither said any of them that ought of the things which he possessed was his own; but they had all things common." The witnesses note the unity and liberality of the early church. The puritan Henry paints the picture: "Behold, how good and how pleasant it was to see how the multitude of those that believed were of one heart, and of one soul... Though there were many, very many, of different ages, tempers, and conditions... yet, when they met in Christ, they were as intimately acquainted as if they had known one another many years... This was the blessed fruit of Christ's dying precept, to love one another, and his dying prayer, that they all might be one."

On "all things common," the witnesses carefully guard against a forced communism.
