The Reformed Calvin refutes the Anabaptists at length: "whereas, under colour of this, the Anabaptists and fantastical men have made much ado, as if there ought to be no civil property of goods amongst Christians, I have already refuted this folly... neither doth Luke prescribe a law to all men which they must of necessity follow... neither doth he speak generally of all men, that it can be gathered that they were not counted Christians which did not sell all that they had." The sharing was voluntary, springing from love, not compelled.

Calvin draws the convicting application: "the faithful did at that day give abundantly even of that which was their own, but we are not only content wickedly to suppress that which we have in our hands, but do also rob others... Love made that common to the poor and needy which was proper to every man." The unity of heart produced the liberality; the inward bond expressed itself in shared goods.

The witnesses agree: the believing multitude was of one heart and soul, holding their goods loosely and sharing all things in common — the blessed fruit of Christ's command to love one another and his prayer that they might be one (Henry). The witnesses carefully guard the "all things common" against any forced communism: it was voluntary, springing from love, not a binding law (Calvin's refutation of the Anabaptists); not all sold all, as Ananias (5:4) and the singling out of Barnabas (v.36) show. The verse displays the ideal of Christian fellowship: hearts so united that none counted his possessions his own, but freely shared with the needy. Calvin draws the convicting contrast with later coldness: where love reigned, goods were common; where love fails, men suppress and rob. The unity of heart is the root, the liberality the fruit. The verse sets before the church the pattern of love that holds earthly goods loosely and shares freely — not by compulsion but by the constraint of a love that makes the many one. Where the Spirit produces one heart, he produces also the open hand.
33:
"And with great power gave the apostles witness of the resurrection of the Lord Jesus: and great grace was upon them all." The witnesses note the apostles' powerful testimony and the grace upon the church. On "great power," Barnes: "The word power here denotes efficacy; and means that they had ability given them to bear witness of the resurrection of the Saviour. It refers, therefore, rather to their preaching, than to their miracles." Henry: "the great vigour, spirit, and courage, with which they published and avowed this doctrine."

On the resurrection as the burden of their witness, Calvin: "Luke maketh mention of the resurrection alone, because it is, as it were, the furnishing or fulfilling of the gospel; and because they had endured a sore combat for the same, and the Sadducees were sore grieved at it." Robertson: "It was on this issue that the Sadducees had arrested them." The resurrection, the heart of the gospel, was the central theme.

On "great grace," the witnesses divide. Some take it as the favour of God upon the disciples (Luke 2:40); others as the favour of the people toward them (Acts 2:47). Ellicott leaves it open. Barnes connects it to their liberality: "their union, their benevolence, their liberality in supplying the wants of the needy, was a means of opening the hearts of the people, and of winning them to the Saviour." Calvin: "they were beloved, because they were beneficial."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles bore witness to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus with great power — efficacy and courage in preaching (Barnes, Henry) — the resurrection being the heart of the gospel and the point on which the Sadducees had attacked them (Calvin, Robertson). And "great grace was upon them all": whether the favour of God upon the disciples or the favour of the people toward them (the witnesses leave it open, Ellicott), connected by Barnes and Calvin to the church's liberality, which won the people's hearts ("beloved, because they were beneficial"). The verse displays the twin marks of the early church: powerful witness to the risen Christ, and abundant grace — God's favour and the people's. The resurrection preached with power, and the love shown in shared goods, together advanced the gospel. The verse links the doctrine (the resurrection) and the life (the liberality): the church that testified to the risen Lord was also the church that loved and gave, and so found grace and favour. The power of the witness and the grace upon the church are the fruit of the Spirit's filling (v.31); and the resurrection remains the central theme of apostolic preaching.
34:
"Neither was there any among them that lacked: for as many as were possessors of lands or houses sold them, and brought the prices of the things that were sold," The witnesses note the practical effect of the church's love — none in want. Gill: "this shows their great charity, and gives a reason why they were in so much favour with the people, because they took so much care of their poor." Barnes guards against pressing the "as many" too far: "it would be improper to press it so as to suppose that every individual that became a Christian sold at once all his property... they parted with whatever property was needful to supply the wants of the poor... That it was by no means considered a matter of obligation is apparent from the case of Ananias (5:4)."

The grammatical voices note the continuous action. Robertson: "Selling they brought from time to time, as there was occasion by reason of need. Hence the wants were kept supplied." Ellicott suggests a further motive: "impressed, by their Lord's warnings of wars and coming troubles, with the instability of earthly possessions... a proof of faith in the future desolation which their Master had foretold."

The Reformed Calvin again guards the voluntary nature: "he meaneth not that the faithful sold all that they had, but only so much as need required... this was the end, that no man might lack. He showeth that they used great wisdom, because it was distributed as every man had need. Therefore the goods were not equally divided, but there was a discreet distribution made, lest any should be out of measure oppressed with poverty." Wesley: "Not that there was any particular command for this; but there was great grace and great love."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the church's love had this practical effect — none among them lacked, for those who had lands or houses sold them and brought the proceeds for the relief of the poor. The witnesses carefully guard the voluntary, need-based nature: not all sold all (the "as many" not pressed), and it was no command but the fruit of grace and love (Barnes, Calvin, Wesley), the sale and distribution made wisely, as need required. The selling was continuous, from time to time as need arose (Robertson); and Ellicott suggests it also showed faith in the coming desolation their Lord had foretold. The verse displays the fruit of the church's unity (v.32): a fellowship in which none was in want, because love freely supplied every need. The liberality was not a forced communism but the spontaneous outflow of grace, distributed with wisdom to relieve the poor. The verse sets the pattern of Christian charity: not the abolition of property but its free use for the needy, so that in the fellowship of love no member lacks. Where great grace is upon a people, none among them lacks; for love supplies what justice could not compel.
35:
"And laid them down at the apostles' feet: and distribution was made unto every man according as he had need." The witnesses note the manner of the giving and the wise distribution. Ellicott describes the Eastern custom: "When gifts or offerings are made to a king, or priest, or teacher, they are not placed in his hands, but at his feet. The Apostles sat, it would seem, in conclave, on their twelve seats, as in the figurative promise of Matthew 19:28." Clarke: "To show how cordially and entirely they parted with them. And they entrusted the management of the whole to those men to whom they found God had entrusted the gifts of his Holy Spirit."

On the distribution, the grammatical voices note it was according to need. Robertson: "Imperfect active of echo with kathoti and an with the notion of customary repetition." Barnes notes the office soon became burdensome: "This soon became a burdensome and inconvenient office, and they therefore appointed men who had especial charge of it (Acts 6:1)."

The wise distribution "according as he had need" the witnesses (with Calvin, under v.34) read as the principle of Christian charity: not an equal division, but a discreet supply of each one's need. The goods were not pooled into a forced equality, but distributed wisely to relieve want — the model of love governed by wisdom.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the proceeds were laid at the apostles' feet — the Eastern gesture of entrusting an offering to a superior (Ellicott), showing how cordially they parted with their goods (Clarke) — and distribution was made to each according to his need. The distribution was governed by need, not equality: a discreet supply of want, the principle of Christian charity (Calvin). The office soon grew burdensome and led to the appointing of the seven (Acts 6:1, Barnes). The verse displays the order and wisdom of the early church's liberality: the gifts entrusted to the apostles, and distributed not by a forced equality but by the measure of each one's need. The "laid at the apostles' feet" marks both the believers' trust and their detachment from their possessions; the "according as he had need" marks the wisdom that governed the love. The verse completes the picture of the church's fellowship: a love that gave freely, an authority that distributed wisely, and a provision that met every need. Christian charity is not the leveling of property but the wise relief of want, that in the body of Christ none should lack.
36:
"And Joses, who by the apostles was surnamed Barnabas, (which is, being interpreted, The son of consolation,) a Levite, and of the country of Cyprus," The witnesses note the introduction of Barnabas as the model of the church's liberality. Chrysostom (whose comment runs into ch. 5) notes the contrast Luke is setting up: "The writer is now about to relate the affair of Ananias and Sapphira, and in order to show that the man's sin was of the worst description, he first mentions him who performed the virtuous deed." Barnabas is the foil to Ananias.

On the name, the grammatical voices unfold its meaning. Barnes: "Barnabas, which is the son of prophecy... The Greek which is used to interpret this, translated consolation, means properly also, exhortation, entreaty, advocacy... it is probable that he possessed it in an eminent degree... on account of his talent for speaking, or exhorting the people to holiness." Robertson: "son of consolation or exhortation... Doubtless his gifts as a preacher lay along this same line... Encouragement is the chief idea in paraklesis." Ellicott connects it to the Paraclete: "The Paraclete had endowed him with the gift of paraclesis."

On his background, the witnesses note he was a Levite of Cyprus. Barnes traces his later career as Paul's companion: "He gave himself, his property, his all, to the service of the Lord Jesus, and went forth to the self-denying labours of the gospel." Ellicott notes the tradition (Clement of Alexandria) that he was one of the Seventy, and his kinship with Mary the mother of John Mark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Luke introduces Barnabas — Joseph the Levite of Cyprus, surnamed by the apostles "son of consolation" (or exhortation) — as the eminent example of the church's liberality, and the foil to Ananias who follows (Chrysostom). The name marks his gift of encouragement and persuasive speech, the work of the Paraclete (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). The verse displays the church's practice of giving honour where due: Barnabas is singled out and named, both for his notable gift and for his coming prominence as Paul's companion and a preacher to the Gentiles. The surname, bestowed by the apostles, witnesses to the gift of the Spirit recognized in him — the gift of consolation and exhortation by which he would build up the church. The verse introduces one of the great figures of the apostolic age, a man who gave his all to Christ; and the singling out of Barbanas (against the supposition that all sold all) confirms that the liberality, though widespread, was voluntary and individual. The son of consolation, named and honoured, models the love that the next chapter's Ananias would counterfeit.
37:
"Having land, sold it, and brought the money, and laid it at the apostles' feet." The witnesses note Barnabas's act of liberality, the concrete example of the church's love. Ellicott discusses the Levite's land: "In the original polity of Israel the Levites had cities and land in common, but no private property... The case of Jeremiah (Jeremiah 32:7-12) shows that there was nothing to hinder priest or Levite from becoming the possessor of land by purchase or inheritance." Whether his land was in Cyprus or Judea is uncertain (Gill).

The grammatical voices note this as the illustration of v.34. Robertson (under v.36): "He is mentioned as one illustration of those in verse 34 who selling brought the money. This fact argues that all did not actually sell, but were ready to do so if needed." Barnabas's act is the named instance of the general practice.

The Reformed Calvin (under v.34) had noted: "peradventure, Joses hath this commendation given him by name, because he sold his only possession. For by this means he passed all the rest." Barnabas's self-chosen poverty, Ellicott adds, "led him afterwards to act as St. Paul did in working for his livelihood (1 Corinthians 9:6)."

The very brief comment matches the brief verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: Barnabas, having land (in Cyprus or Judea, uncertain), sold it and laid the money at the apostles' feet — the concrete, named example of the church's liberality described in v.34. A Levite might own land by purchase or inheritance (as Jeremiah did, Ellicott); and Barnabas's act, perhaps the sale of his only possession (Calvin), surpassed the rest and won him the honourable mention. His self-chosen poverty would later lead him, like Paul, to work for his own living (Ellicott, 1 Corinthians 9:6). The verse closes the chapter with the model of Christian liberality embodied in a man: not a forced communism, but the free gift of one who loved Christ and his church. Barnabas stands as the foil to Ananias (ch. 5): the true giver against the false. His laying the money at the apostles' feet, like the whole church's practice, was the spontaneous fruit of grace — love freely sharing earthly goods for the relief of the needy and the glory of Christ. The son of consolation consoles by his gift; and the church's love finds its concrete expression in his open hand.
